title: The Super Nabla Operator
overall_summary: The paper defines the super nabla operator ∇_* by declaring the modified Macdonald polynomials Htilde_μ to be joint eigenvectors with tensor-valued eigenvalue Htilde_μ itself, i.e. ∇_* Htilde_μ = Htilde_μ ⊗ Htilde_μ. It shows that many standard Macdonald eigenoperators, including nabla, Delta operators, and Π, are obtained from ∇_* by scalar products or plethystic specializations. The main combinatorial work specializes t=1 and converts expansions of iterated super nabla operators into positive area enumerators of multi-labeled Dyck paths and marked-return variants, using forgotten symmetric functions, column-composition tableaux, and sign-reversing involutions. The most relevant output for the original problem is the Schur expansion of ∇_*^k e_n at t=1 in terms of lattice multi-labeled Dyck paths, together with operator identities expressing Ξ e_λ and Ξ s_λ through scalar products against ∇_*^k e_n. These tools are plausibly useful whenever universal Schur coefficients of multigraded Hilbert series can be recast as coefficients or scalar products of Macdonald-eigenoperator expressions.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 1.1 and Proposition 1.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let Λ be the ring of symmetric functions over Q(q,t), and let Htilde_μ(x;q,t) denote the modified Macdonald polynomial indexed by a partition μ. Define the super nabla operator ∇_*: Λ → Λ ⊗ Λ by ∇_* Htilde_μ = Htilde_μ ⊗ Htilde_μ; equivalently, for a second variable set y, ∇_y Htilde_μ(x) = Htilde_μ(y) Htilde_μ(x). For a symmetric function F, define the scalar-product specialization ⟨F,∇_*⟩ by ⟨F,∇_*⟩ Htilde_μ = ⟨F,Htilde_μ⟩ Htilde_μ, where ⟨ , ⟩ is the Hall inner product. For a cell c=(i,j) of μ in French coordinates, set B_μ(q,t)=Σ_{(i,j) in μ} q^i t^j and Π_μ(q,t)=Π_{(i,j) in μ, (i,j)≠(0,0)}(1-q^i t^j). Let Δ_{e_k} be the Macdonald eigenoperator Δ_{e_k} Htilde_μ = e_k[B_μ] Htilde_μ, and let Π be the eigenoperator Π Htilde_μ = Π_μ Htilde_μ. Then for every homogeneous symmetric function G of degree n, ⟨p_n,∇_*⟩G = ΠG and ⟨e_k h_{n-k},∇_*⟩G = Δ_{e_k}G.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: It suffices to compare eigenvalues on the Macdonald basis. The key evaluation is Htilde_μ[1-u] = Π_{(i,j) in μ}(1-u q^i t^j). On the other hand, for any homogeneous F of degree n, plethystic Cauchy reproduction gives F[1-u] = ⟨F[X], h_n[X(1-u)]⟩, and h_n[X(1-u)] = Σ_{k=0}^n (-u)^k e_k[X]h_{n-k}[X]. Comparing coefficients of (-u)^k gives ⟨F,e_k h_{n-k}⟩, so for F=Htilde_μ this coefficient is e_k[B_μ]. Thus ⟨e_k h_{n-k},Htilde_μ⟩=e_k[B_μ]. For p_n, divide Htilde_μ[1-u] by 1-u and set u=1; because the cell (0,0) contributes the factor 1-u, the result is Π_μ. In the power-sum expansion of F[1-u], after division by 1-u and specialization u=1 only the partition (n) survives, yielding exactly ⟨F,p_n⟩. Hence ⟨p_n,Htilde_μ⟩=Π_μ. These eigenvalue identities prove the operator statements.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Specialization identities in Section 3.3, especially Equations (3.15)--(3.20)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For the plethystic sign alphabet ε satisfying p_r[ε]=(-1)^r, the modified Macdonald polynomials satisfy Htilde_μ[1-u] = Π_{(i,j) in μ}(1-u q^i t^j) and equivalently Htilde_μ[1-εu] = Π_{(i,j) in μ}(1+u q^i t^j). Consequently, on homogeneous degree n symmetric functions, ∇_1 is the identity, ∇_{-1}=(-1)^n ∇, and ∇_{-ε}=∇, where ∇ is the usual nabla operator with eigenvalue T_μ=Π_{(i,j) in μ}q^i t^j. Also ∇_{1-εu}=Σ_{k≥0}u^k Δ_{e_k}; more generally, applying two independent such specializations to ∇_* gives ∇_{1-εu}∇_{1-εv}=Σ_{k,j≥0}u^k v^j Δ_{e_k e_j}. The paper notes that all Δ_{e_λ}, hence all Δ_F by linearity, can be obtained from suitable coefficients of iterated super nabla specializations, and Π is obtained on nonconstant symmetric functions as lim_{u→1}(1-u)^{-1}∇_{1-u}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: All statements follow by evaluating eigenvalues on Htilde_μ. The identity Htilde_μ[1-εu]=Π_c(1+u q^{i(c)}t^{j(c)}) expands as Σ_k u^k e_k[B_μ], so ∇_{1-εu} has eigenvalue Σ_k u^k e_k[B_μ], exactly the generating series of Δ_{e_k}. Two independent specializations multiply eigenvalues, producing e_k[B_μ]e_j[B_μ] and hence Δ_{e_k e_j}. The specializations at 1, -1, and -ε are obtained by plugging those alphabets into Htilde_μ and using the known evaluations Htilde_μ[1]=1, Htilde_μ[-1]=(-1)^{|μ|}T_μ, and Htilde_μ[-ε]=T_μ. For Π, the factor corresponding to the cell (0,0) produces a simple zero in ∇_{1-u}; dividing by 1-u and taking u→1 leaves the product over all nonzero cells.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.4 and Corollary 3.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a partition μ of n, let R(μ) be the set of rearrangements α=(α_1,...,α_ℓ) of the parts of μ. A column-composition tableau of type μ is a pair C=(α,c), where α∈R(μ), c=(c_1,...,c_n) is a weakly increasing sequence of nonnegative integers, and c_i<c_{i+1} implies i is a partial sum of α. Let CC_all_μ be the set of all such C and CC_zero_μ the subset with c_1=0. Let |C|=c_1+...+c_n, and define generating functions CC_all_μ(q)=Σ_{C in CC_all_μ}q^{|C|} and CC_zero_μ(q)=Σ_{C in CC_zero_μ}q^{|C|}. If f_μ is the forgotten symmetric function and Fhat denotes plethystic substitution F[X/(1-q)], then fhat_μ(1)=(-1)^{|μ|-ℓ(μ)} CC_all_μ(q), and (1-q^{|μ|})fhat_μ(1)=(-1)^{|μ|-ℓ(μ)} CC_zero_μ(q). In particular, for μ=(1^n), f_{1^n}=h_n and hhat_n(1)=1/((1-q)(1-q^2)...(1-q^n)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The starting point is the Egecioglu-Remmel combinatorial formula f_μ(x)=(-1)^{n-ℓ(μ)}Σ_{α∈R(μ)}Σ_{i_1≤...≤i_ℓ}x_{i_1}^{α_1}...x_{i_ℓ}^{α_ℓ}. Plethystically substituting X=1/(1-q), i.e. variables 1,q,q^2,..., yields a sum over weakly increasing indices with q-weight i_1α_1+...+i_ℓα_ℓ. Replacing each part α_j by a block of α_j columns all having height i_j gives exactly a column-composition tableau, and the exponent is the total column height |C|. This proves the formula for CC_all. Multiplying by 1-q^n shifts all column heights simultaneously and cancels all configurations with positive first height, leaving precisely the c_1=0 subset. For μ=(1^n), the forgotten function is h_n, giving the classical principal specialization of h_n.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Macdonald Cauchy identities and Equation (3.14)
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let M=(1-q)(1-t), let F^*(X)=F[X/M], and let w_μ(q,t)=Π_{c in μ}(q^{a(c)}-t^{ℓ(c)+1})(t^{ℓ(c)}-q^{a(c)+1}), where a(c) and ℓ(c) are the arm and leg of c. The modified Macdonald polynomials satisfy the q,t-Cauchy identity e_n^*[XY]=Σ_{μ⊢n} Htilde_μ(X;q,t)Htilde_μ(Y;q,t)/w_μ(q,t). Using the evaluation Htilde_μ[M]=M B_μ(q,t)Π_μ(q,t), the elementary symmetric function has the Macdonald expansion e_n(X)=Σ_{μ⊢n} M B_μ Π_μ Htilde_μ(X;q,t)/w_μ(q,t). Equivalently, e_n=M Δ_{e_1} Π e_n^*.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The q,t-Cauchy identity is the standard reproducing identity for the modified Macdonald basis with respect to the q,t scalar product for which ⟨Htilde_λ,Htilde_μ⟩_* = δ_{λμ}w_μ. Substituting the alphabet M into one side of the kernel gives e_n[M X/M]=e_n[X]. On the right side, the substituted Macdonald polynomial evaluates to Htilde_μ[M]=M B_μΠ_μ. This immediately gives the stated expansion of e_n in the Macdonald basis. Since e_n^*=Σ_μ Htilde_μ/w_μ and the operators Δ_{e_1} and Π multiply Htilde_μ by B_μ and Π_μ, respectively, the operator form e_n=MΔ_{e_1}Πe_n^* follows.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.8 and Proposition 3.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Define Ξ:Λ→Λ by ΞF(X)=M Δ_{e_1} Π F[X/M]. Then for every partition λ of n and every k≥0, ∇_*^k Ξ e_λ = ⟨h_λ,∇_*⟩ ∇_*^k e_n, and ∇_*^k Ξ s_λ = ⟨s_λ,∇_*⟩ ∇_*^k e_n. Here e_λ, h_λ, and s_λ are elementary, complete, and Schur symmetric functions, and the scalar product in ⟨h_λ,∇_*⟩ or ⟨s_λ,∇_*⟩ is the ordinary Hall scalar product applied to the Macdonald eigenvalue Htilde_μ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Use the Macdonald expansion of e_n from Equation (3.14). Orthogonality for the q,t scalar product gives Ξe_λ = Σ_{μ⊢n} (M B_μΠ_μ/w_μ) Htilde_μ ⟨e_λ^*,Htilde_μ⟩_*. The relation between the q,t scalar product and the Hall scalar product converts ⟨e_λ^*,Htilde_μ⟩_* into ⟨h_λ,Htilde_μ⟩. Therefore Ξe_λ is obtained from the Macdonald expansion of e_n by multiplying each Htilde_μ coefficient by the eigenvalue ⟨h_λ,Htilde_μ⟩, i.e. Ξe_λ=⟨h_λ,∇_*⟩e_n. The same argument with the self-duality of Schur functions gives Ξs_λ=⟨s_λ,∇_*⟩e_n. Since all these operators are diagonal in the Htilde_μ basis, they commute with ∇_* and its iterates, proving the displayed identities.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Definition 3.10, Equation (3.26), and the t=1 specialization framework
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: At t=1, the modified Macdonald polynomial specializes to Htilde_μ(X;q,1)=hhat_μ(X)/hhat_μ(1), where hhat_μ(X)=h_μ[X/(1-q)]. Define b∇_*:Λ_{Q(q)}→Λ_{Q(q)}⊗Λ_{Q(q)} by b∇_* hhat_μ = hhat_μ⊗hhat_μ / hhat_μ(1). If F has no pole at t=1, then (∇_*F)|_{t=1}=b∇_*(F|_{t=1}). For n≥1 and k≥0, b∇_*^k e_n = Σ_{μ⊢n} f_μ[1-q] hhat_μ(1) (hhat_μ/hhat_μ(1))^{⊗(k+1)}. The paper also uses f_μ[1-q]=(-1)^{n-ℓ(μ)}Σ_{β∈R(μ)}(1-q^{β_1}). Finally, the monomial coefficients of hhat_μ/hhat_μ(1) are described by the t=1 specialization of the Haglund-Haiman-Loehr formula: for a composition β rearranging μ and a word α with prescribed multiplicities, the weight is q^{revmaj_β(α)}, where revmaj_β is the sum, over the blocks of β, of the reverse major index of the corresponding subword.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The specialization Htilde_μ(X;q,1)=hhat_μ(X)/hhat_μ(1) is standard and makes the t=1 version of ∇_* a diagonal operator on the hhat_μ basis with the displayed tensor eigenvalue. Expanding e_n=e_n[(1-q)X/(1-q)] in the dual forgotten-complete bases gives e_n=Σ_{μ⊢n} f_μ[1-q]hhat_μ. Applying b∇_* repeatedly to each hhat_μ produces one tensor copy per iteration and the normalization hhat_μ(1), giving Equation (3.26). The specialization formula for f_μ[1-q] follows either by taking the t=1 limit of M B_μΠ_μ/w_μ or by summation formulas; it is also consistent with the column-composition tableau formula. The HHL formula at t=1 gives the revmaj expansion of each normalized hhat_μ factor, which is the input for the later signed tableau and Dyck-path models.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 3.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For n≥1 and k≥0, b∇_*^k e_n admits a preliminary expansion with one elementary tensor factor. Let PR(η,μ) be the set of sequences of partitions ν_vec=(ν_1,...,ν_r) whose part sizes are grouped according to a rearrangement of μ and whose collective multiset of parts rearranges to η. For a sequence ν_vec set fhat_{ν_vec}(1)=Π_i fhat_{ν_i}(1). Then b∇_*^k e_n = Σ_{η⊢n} e_η ⊗ (Σ_{μ⊢n} (-1)^{n-ℓ(μ)}Σ_{β∈R(μ)}(1-q^{β_1})Σ_w q^{revmaj_β(w)} X^w Σ_{ν_vec∈PR(η,μ)} fhat_{ν_vec}(1)), where w runs over words of k-tuples of label indices, revmaj_β(w) is the sum of the reverse major indices in each coordinate and β-block, and X^w is the corresponding monomial in the k remaining alphabets.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Start from the t=1 spectral expansion in Equation (3.26). Expand one tensor factor hhat_μ in the elementary basis by Cauchy duality: hhat_μ = Σ_{η⊢n} e_η Σ_{ν_vec∈PR(η,μ)} fhat_{ν_vec}(1). Expand the remaining normalized hhat_μ/hhat_μ(1) tensor factors in monomials using the t=1 HHL formula, producing q^{revmaj_β(w)}. Substitute the explicit expression f_μ[1-q]=(-1)^{n-ℓ(μ)}Σ_{β∈R(μ)}(1-q^{β_1}). Collecting terms gives the displayed formula. This expansion is not manifestly positive; Section 4 provides the sign-reversing involution that turns its signed rational pieces into area enumerators.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Section 4 fixed-point theorem: Propositions 4.6, 4.10, 4.15, and Corollary 4.16
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix an alphabet X, a set W of words in X, a ring A, and a weight function ρ:W→A[q] that looks right, meaning ρ(w)=Π_{i=1}^{ℓ(w)} q^{lwt(w,i)(ℓ(w)-i)} wt(w_i), where wt:X→A and lwt(w,i) is a nonnegative integer depending only on the adjacent letters w_i and w_{i+1}. For a partition η⊢n, define a signed set LC_{W,η} of sequences of W-labeled column-composition tableaux of type η, with signs given by the parity of the total number of vertical bars and weights q^{column area}ρ(labels). Let D_{W,η}(q)=Σ_{T∈LC_{W,η}} sign(T)weight(T). Then D_{W,η}(q) equals the positive weight generating function over ρ-compatible W-labeled parallelogram polyominoes of type η: D_{W,η}(q)=Σ_{P∈P_{W,η}}q^{area(P)}wt(P). Here a parallelogram polyomino is a pair of nonintersecting north-east lattice paths P,Q with common endpoints, its area is the number of cells between them minus the minimal m+n-1, type η means the maximal vertical run lengths of the top path rearrange to η, and ρ-compatibility means the bottom path has exactly lwt(w,i)+δ_{i,1} east steps on the line y=i-1.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The proof has two stages. First, a sign-reversing involution is built on labeled column-composition tableau sequences. If a tableau S has a vertical bar, split(S) cuts at the first bar and adds d=Σ lwt(w,i) over the left part to every column of the right part. Proposition 4.6 verifies exact weight preservation: the column-area factor changes by q^{d times the length of the right part}, while the factor ρ decomposes into the two subwords with precisely the inverse q-power, because ρ looks right. Lemma 4.7 gives the inverse join criterion, namely the second piece can be joined to the first exactly when its first column height is at least the last height of the first piece plus the corresponding local-weight sum; Lemma 4.8 ensures that recursive choices are compatible. The involution ψ scans from the left, splitting the first splittable component, joining the first joinable adjacent pair, or recursing. Its fixed points are precisely sequences with no vertical bars and no allowed adjacent joins, equivalently sequences T=(T_i) satisfying c(T_{i+1}) < c(T_i)+Σ_{j in block i}lwt(w,j). Second, Proposition 4.15 constructs a bijection φ from these fixed points to compatible parallelogram polyominoes. For fixed block lengths β_i, heights c(T_i), and labels w, set s_i=c(T_i)+Σ_{j in block i}lwt(w,j)-c(T_{i+1}); then define the top path P=(Π_i N^{β_i}E^{s_i})E and bottom path Q=E(Π_{j=1}^n E^{lwt(w,j)}N). The fixed-point inequalities make all s_i positive and guarantee P stays above Q. A direct row-by-row calculation shows that the polyomino area equals the original tableau area plus the local-weight exponents in ρ. This proves the positive formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let k,n be positive integers. A multi-labeled kn-Dyck path in LD_{kn} is a pair L=(π,w), where π is a lattice path from (0,0) to (kn,n) with north and east steps staying weakly above the diagonal x=ky, and w=(w_1,...,w_n) labels the north steps by (k+1)-tuples w_i=(w_{i0},...,w_{ik}) of positive integers. If s_i=#{j:0≤j≤k and w_{ij}≥w_{i+1,j}} is the number of weak descents between consecutive label tuples, then the i-th north step of π must be followed by at least s_i east steps, equivalently s_i≤col_π(i+1)-col_π(i). Let area(π) be the number of whole cells between π and x=ky, and let X^w=Π_{i=1}^nΠ_{j=0}^k x_{j,w_{ij}}, interpreted tensorially in k+1 alphabets. Then b∇_*^k e_n = Σ_{(π,w)∈LD_{kn}} q^{area(π)} X^w.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Choose W to be the set of all words in the alphabet of positive (k+1)-tuples, set lwt(w,i)=#{j: w_{ij}<w_{i+1,j}}, the number of strict ascents, and set wt(w_i)=Π_j x_{j,w_{ij}}. With these choices, the revmaj statistics from the t=1 HHL expansion are exactly of the looks-right form required in Section 4. The signed expansion from Proposition 3.11 is therefore transformed by Corollary 4.16 into a positive sum over compatible labeled parallelogram polyominoes. The map ι of Proposition 5.7 converts those polyominoes into the stated multi-labeled rectangular Dyck paths by inserting east steps according to the non-ascents, i.e. weak descents, of the label tuples. The area is preserved by this insertion-deletion process, and the monomial label weight is unchanged.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.7
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Fix a partition λ⊢n and a partition η⊢n. Let W_λ be the set of words w=(w_1,...,w_n) of (k+1)-tuples such that the first coordinate word w_{10}...w_{n0} has multiplicity type 1^{λ_1}2^{λ_2}... and the remaining coordinates are positive. Let LD_{kn}(λ,η) be the subset of multi-labeled kn-Dyck paths with this first-coordinate content and with e-composition η, where i is a partial sum of η exactly when #{j: w_{ij}≥w_{i+1,j}} < col_π(i+1)-col_π(i). Let P_{W_λ,η} be the set of ρ-compatible W_λ-labeled parallelogram polyominoes of type η for the local weight lwt=#{coordinatewise strict ascents}. The map ι sends L=(π,w) to a parallelogram polyomino by taking P=πE and Q=E(E^kN)^n, then for each 0≤i<n removing #{j:w_{ij}≥w_{i+1,j}} east steps of P on the line y=i and the same number of east steps of Q on the line y=i-1. This defines a weight-preserving bijection LD_{kn}(λ,η) ↔ P_{W_λ,η}; in particular area(L)=area(ι(L)).
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Dyck-path label condition ensures that there are enough east steps after the i-th north step of π to remove the required number of non-ascents. Removing the same number of east steps from the bottom path on the row below changes neither the relative area contribution of that row nor the label word. After removal, the bottom path has exactly k-#{weak descents}+δ_{i,1}=#{strict ascents}+δ_{i,1}=lwt(w,i)+δ_{i,1} east steps on row y=i-1, so the polyomino is ρ-compatible. The definition of e-composition records precisely the rows where at least one east step remains in the top path after the deletion, hence the vertical run lengths of the top path have type η. The construction is reversible by reinserting the deleted east steps in both paths, so it is bijective, and the equal deletion in adjacent rows proves area preservation.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Corollaries 5.4 and 5.5
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For L=(π,w) with k coordinate labels rather than k+1, define η(L) to be the composition of n whose partial sums are the indices i such that #{j: w_{ij}≥w_{i+1,j}} < col_π(i+1)-col_π(i). Let w(L) be the k-coordinate label word and X^{w(L)} its tensor monomial. Then b∇_*^k e_n = Σ_{L∈LD^k_{kn}} q^{area(L)} e_{η(L)} ⊗ X^{w(L)}. Moreover, if λ⊢n and LD_{kn}(λ) denotes the multi-labeled paths in LD_{kn} whose first coordinate word has multiplicity type 1^{λ_1}2^{λ_2}..., then ∇_*^k Ξ e_λ evaluated at t=1 equals Σ_{L∈LD_{kn}(λ)} q^{area(L)} e_{η(L)} ⊗ X^{w(L)}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For the first formula, start from the monomial expansion with k+1 label coordinates and group the first coordinate labels. Given π and the last k coordinates, the allowed first-coordinate words are exactly encoded by the elementary symmetric factor e_{η(L)}: a break in η(L) occurs precisely when the path has surplus east steps beyond those forced by the weak descents in the last k coordinates, allowing the first-coordinate label to reset without violating the multi-label condition. For the second formula, apply Proposition 3.9, which identifies Ξe_λ at t=1 with the scalar-product specialization ⟨h_λ,∇_*⟩e_n. Taking this scalar product in the first tensor coordinate of the monomial formula extracts exactly the first-coordinate words of content λ, producing the displayed restricted sum.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Theorem 5.8
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: A word a_1...a_n of positive integers is a lattice word of type λ if every prefix contains at least as many r's as (r+1)'s for every r, and the multiplicities form the partition λ. Let LLD_{kn} be the subset of the paths in the elementary expansion of Corollary 5.4 for which, in each of the k label coordinates, the coordinate word is a lattice word; write λ_j(w(L)) for the type of the j-th coordinate word. Then b∇_*^k e_n = Σ_{L∈LLD_{kn}} q^{area(L)} e_{η(L)} ⊗ s_{λ_1(w(L))} ⊗ ... ⊗ s_{λ_k(w(L))}.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: The Schur expansion of hhat_β/hhat_β(1) at t=1 is obtained from the same HHL specialization by restricting monomial label words to lattice words: hhat_β/hhat_β(1)=Σ_{λ⊢n}s_λ Σ_{α∈LW_λ}q^{revmaj_β(α)}, where LW_λ is the set of lattice words of type λ. Thus one repeats the Section 4 sign-reversing involution with W equal to the set of k-tuples of lattice words of specified types and with the same local ascent weight. The fixed-point and polyomino-to-Dyck bijections are unchanged, and the lattice-word restriction converts each monomial tensor factor into the corresponding Schur function s_{λ_j}. This yields the displayed Schur-positive q-area formula at t=1.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 5.9
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: The super nabla image of e_n satisfies ∇_* e_n = Σ_λ m_λ ⊗ Ξ e_λ, where the sum is over partitions λ of n and m_λ is the monomial symmetric function.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: View ∇_*e_n as ∇_Y e_n(X), a symmetric function in two independent alphabets. Expanding in the monomial basis in the Y variables gives coefficients obtained by Hall scalar products with the dual complete basis h_λ. Proposition 3.9 gives ⟨h_λ,∇_*⟩e_n=Ξe_λ. Hence the coefficient of m_λ(Y) is Ξe_λ(X), proving the expansion.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Proposition 6.2
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: For a partition μ, let SR(μ) be the set of selected rearrangements obtained by choosing a rearrangement α=(α_1,...,α_{ℓ(μ)}) of the parts of μ and then marking one cell in the first row α_1. Thus |SR(μ)|=Σ_{α∈R(μ)}α_1. Newton's identity is expressed as p_n = Σ_{μ⊢n} (-1)^{ℓ(μ)-1}|SR(μ)| h_μ. Equivalently, after plethystic substitution X/(1-q), p_n[X/(1-q)] = p_n(X)/(1-q^n) = -Σ_{μ⊢n}(-1)^{ℓ(μ)}|SR(μ)| hhat_μ, and applying ω gives ω(p_n)=(1-q^n)Σ_{μ⊢n}(-1)^{n-ℓ(μ)}|SR(μ)|hhat_μ.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: This is Newton's identity rewritten in the complete basis and with a combinatorial interpretation of the coefficient. The coefficient |SR(μ)| records both the choice of an ordered rearrangement of the parts and the choice of a distinguished cell in the first selected part, so it equals Σ_{α∈R(μ)}α_1. The plethystic version follows from p_n[X/(1-q)]=p_n(X)/(1-q^n) and h_μ[X/(1-q)]=hhat_μ. Applying the involution ω changes p_n by the standard sign (-1)^{n-1}, giving the final displayed formula.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 6.3, 6.5, 6.10, and 6.11
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let γ be a nonempty partition, and let bΔ_{m_γ} denote the t=1 specialization of the Delta operator, acting diagonally on hhat_μ by the eigenvalue m_γ[Σ_i[μ_i]_q]. The paper expands b∇_*^k bΔ_{m_γ}ω(p_n) as a signed sum over selected labeled column-composition tableaux. An element S of SC_γ(η,μ) is built by choosing a selected rearrangement α∈SR(μ), a partition vector ν_vec∈PR(η,α), column-composition tableaux C_i of types ν_i with at least one first column of height 0, a circle inherited from the selected cell of α_1, and labels by (k+1)-tuples whose zeroth coordinate word rearranges γ together with zeros and whose other coordinates are positive. The weight is q^{column area} times q^{rw of zeroth labels} times the coordinatewise q^{revmaj} monomial weight, and the sign is (-1)^{ℓ(η)-ℓ(μ)}. Proposition 6.5 states b∇_*^k bΔ_{m_γ}ω(p_n)=Σ_{η⊢n}Σ_{μ⊢n}Σ_{S∈SC_γ(η,μ)} sign(S)ρ(S)⊗e_η. Proposition 6.10 defines a weight-preserving sign-reversing involution ψ_circle on these objects, and Proposition 6.11 concludes that only the fixed points SM^γ_η remain: b∇_*^k bΔ_{m_γ}ω(p_n)=Σ_{η⊢n}Σ_{S∈SM^γ_η}ρ(S)⊗e_η.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: Proposition 6.3 starts from the ω(p_n) expansion in Proposition 6.2, applies bΔ_{m_γ} to hhat_μ using the t=1 eigenvalue m_γ[Σ_i[μ_i]_q], and applies b∇_*^k using the normalized hhat_μ tensor expansion. The factor (1-q^n) together with the forgotten-function principal specializations is interpreted as sequences of column-composition tableaux with at least one component whose first column has height 0. The selected cell in SR(μ) becomes the circle, and the Delta eigenvalue m_γ[Σ_i[μ_i]_q] becomes the zeroth-coordinate label word with content γ plus zeros, weighted by rw. This gives the signed tableau formula of Proposition 6.5. The involution ψ_circle modifies the Section 4 split-join involution to handle the circle cyclically. If the circle is not leading, split at the rightmost vertical bar to its left and rotate the left piece to the end. If the circle is leading and the last component can join the first, perform that cyclic join. Otherwise apply the ordinary ψ from Section 4. Splitting and joining preserve weight by the same local-weight calculation as Proposition 4.6 and reverse sign by changing the number of bars by one. The only delicate case is when there is a single component; the assumption |γ|>0 ensures that after splitting, a nonzero zeroth label forces enough added column height to prevent the image from falling into the cyclic-join case. Thus ψ_circle is an involution, and its fixed points are exactly the no-bar configurations with no ordinary or cyclic joins and with the circle leading.

key_theorems_lemmas.label: Propositions 6.14, 6.16, and 6.18
key_theorems_lemmas.statement: Let γ be a nonempty partition. A ρ-compatible polyomino with a marked return is obtained from the fixed points SM^γ_η by a bijection φ_circle: rotate the fixed-point sequence so that a component with first-column height 0 comes first, apply the Section 4 polyomino map φ, and keep the circle as a mark on a return before the first return of the paths. Proposition 6.14 states that φ_circle is weight-preserving and bijective onto a set RP^γ_{k,η} of compatible labeled parallelogram polyominoes with marked return and with the top path ending in two east steps. Applying the deletion-insertion map ι gives multi-labeled (γ,kn)-Dyck paths with a marked return. In these paths the bottom path has the form Q=E E^{a_1}N ... E^{a_n}N with (a_1-k,...,a_n-k) a rearrangement of γ together with n-ℓ(γ) zeros, the top path satisfies the label descent condition, and a return row is marked before the first return. With tensor order as in Proposition 6.16, b∇_*^k bΔ_{m_γ}ω(p_n)=Σ_{L∈RLD^γ_{kn}}q^{area(L)}X^L⊗e_{η(L)}. The Schur version, Proposition 6.18, restricts labels to coordinatewise lattice words and gives b∇_*^k bΔ_{m_γ}ω(p_n)=Σ_{L∈RLLD^γ_{kn}}q^{area(L)}e_{η(L)}⊗s_{λ_1(w(L))}⊗...⊗s_{λ_k(w(L))}, with the paper also presenting equivalent tensor-order variants.
key_theorems_lemmas.proof_sketch: For a fixed point S=(S_1,...,S_r), if c(S_1)=0 apply φ directly; if not, choose the largest j with c(S_j)=0, cyclically rotate to (S_j,...,S_r,S_1,...,S_{j-1}), and apply φ. The no-join condition around the chosen zero-height component implies that either the preceding height or the relevant local weight is positive, which forces the resulting top path to end with two east steps. The circle lies in the initial block after rotation and becomes a marked row before the first return. Weight preservation is inherited from φ and from the fact that cyclic rotation is compensated by the cyclic join inequalities in the fixed-point set. Then the map ι removes or inserts east steps according to coordinatewise label non-ascents exactly as in Proposition 5.7; because the zeroth labels now encode the parts of γ, the bottom path becomes the specified (γ,kn)-staircase. The Schur formula is obtained by replacing arbitrary positive label words in the k nonzero coordinates by lattice words, using the same lattice-word Schur expansion as in Theorem 5.8.

proof_techniques.name: Macdonald eigenoperator extraction by scalar product
proof_techniques.description: The paper treats ∇_* as a universal Macdonald eigenoperator whose eigenvalue on Htilde_μ is the entire symmetric function Htilde_μ in a second alphabet. Pairing this eigenvalue with a chosen symmetric function F produces the ordinary scalar eigenoperator with eigenvalue ⟨F,Htilde_μ⟩. Evaluations such as Htilde_μ[1-u]=Π_c(1-uq^i t^j) then identify these scalar products with standard eigenvalues such as e_k[B_μ] and Π_μ.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If the Hilbert series coefficients c_λ(n) can be expressed as scalar products against a Macdonald-eigenoperator formula, hook shapes may be attacked by choosing F to be a hook Schur function or a related e_a h_b expression and then translating the scalar product into a restriction on labels.

proof_techniques.name: Reduction through the Ξ basis
proof_techniques.description: The operator ΞF=MΔ_{e_1}ΠF[X/M] is related to ∇_* by Ξe_λ=⟨h_λ,∇_*⟩e_n and Ξs_λ=⟨s_λ,∇_*⟩e_n. Thus all expressions ∇_*^kΞe_λ and ∇_*^kΞs_λ are obtained from ∇_*^{k+1}e_n by taking an ordinary Hall scalar product in one tensor factor.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is useful if the coinvariant Hilbert series is known or suspected to involve Θ or Ξ operators, since hook Schur coefficients can then be reduced to controlled coefficient extraction from the master object ∇_*^{k+1}e_n.

proof_techniques.name: t=1 degeneration to complete symmetric functions
proof_techniques.description: At t=1, Htilde_μ becomes h_μ[X/(1-q)] divided by its principal specialization. This turns Macdonald eigenoperator calculations into complete-basis calculations and makes the HHL formula collapse to reverse-major-index weights. The expansion of e_n in the hhat_μ basis uses forgotten symmetric functions evaluated at 1-q.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: If a hook coefficient problem has a specialization or filtration whose Hilbert series is governed by t=1 Macdonald theory, this degeneration replaces difficult two-parameter Macdonald combinatorics by q-enumeration of words, descents, and tableaux.

proof_techniques.name: Forgotten functions as column-composition tableaux
proof_techniques.description: Principal specializations of forgotten symmetric functions are interpreted as signed generating functions for column-composition tableaux. The factor 1-q^n imposes a zero first column condition. These tableaux encode the negative signs appearing in complete-basis expansions before cancellation.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This provides a way to convert alternating symmetric-function sums, which often arise from hook or power-sum expansions, into signed combinatorial objects amenable to involution.

proof_techniques.name: Looks-right local weights and split-join involution
proof_techniques.description: A weight ρ on words looks right when it factors as local adjacent contributions q^{lwt(w,i)(n-i)} times letter weights. This exact form allows a split operation at a vertical bar to preserve weight: added column area on the right is canceled by the change in local q-exponents. A recursively defined split-join involution cancels all non-fixed signed column-composition tableau sequences.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: For hook coefficients, one can try to encode hook restrictions as local label conditions while preserving a looks-right weight. If successful, the same involution can turn an alternating algebraic coefficient formula into a positive path model.

proof_techniques.name: Fixed points to parallelogram polyominoes
proof_techniques.description: After cancellation, fixed points are sequences with no vertical bars and no joinable adjacent pair. Their heights and local weights determine a top path P and bottom path Q of a parallelogram polyomino by explicit formulas, and a direct area computation proves that the original q-weight is q^{area(P,Q)} times label weight.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This is a general positive-combinatorics mechanism: once an algebraic coefficient is expressed in the paper's D_{W,η} form, the coefficient becomes an area generating function over compatible polyominoes.

proof_techniques.name: Deleting east steps to pass from polyominoes to multi-labeled Dyck paths
proof_techniques.description: The bijection ι starts from a rectangular Dyck path with tuple labels and deletes, in both the top and bottom paths, east steps corresponding to coordinatewise weak descents. Compatibility of the polyomino bottom path becomes the Dyck-path label condition that enough east steps follow each north step. Since equal deletions are made in adjacent rows, area is preserved.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: This converts polyomino fixed-point models into more familiar Dyck-path models. Hook coefficients might be expressible as counts of paths with special label content or reading-word restrictions.

proof_techniques.name: Schur extraction via lattice words
proof_techniques.description: The Schur expansion is obtained by restricting monomial label words to lattice words. A lattice word records the row word of a standard tableau, and summing over lattice words of type λ converts monomial expansions into Schur functions s_λ.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Since the original problem asks for Schur coefficients c_λ(n), especially for hook λ, this technique suggests interpreting such coefficients by imposing hook-type lattice-word conditions on one or more label coordinates.

proof_techniques.name: Marked-return cyclic involution for power sums
proof_techniques.description: For power-sum inputs, Newton's identity introduces a selected cell in the first part of a rearrangement. The ordinary split-join involution is modified cyclically around this selected cell: nonleading circles are handled by splitting and rotating, while leading circles allow a cyclic join of the last and first components. Fixed points become polyominoes or Dyck paths with a marked return.
proof_techniques.applicability_to_problem: Hook Schur functions have close relationships with e-h combinations and power-sum expansions; if a hook coefficient is transformed into a power-sum or cyclically selected expression, the marked-return model may provide the needed combinatorial interpretation.

other_useful_info: The paper notes that the family Ξe_λ is a basis of Λ and that Ξe_{λ,1}=Δ_{e_1}Θ_{e_λ}e_1 in terms of Theta operators, so understanding ∇_*^{k+1}e_n is enough to understand ∇_*^k applied to any Ξe_λ. Section 5.3 explains that when k=1, scalar products of the two-label Dyck-path formula recover familiar t=1 forms of the shuffle theorem and the valley Delta conjecture: pairing with e_n forces one label coordinate to be strictly increasing in reading order, while pairing with e_{n-k}h_k corresponds to decorated valleys. Proposition 5.9 combined with D'Adderio-Iraci-Le Borgne-Romero-Vanden Wyngaerd is said to give a rooted tiered-tree and tree-inversion interpretation for an m⊗m expansion of ∇_*e_n at t=1; the same cited work relates coefficients of Ξe_{1^n} to Kac polynomials of certain dandelion quivers. Section 6.4 observes that the γ=1^n, k=0 case of the marked-return formula coincides at t=1 with the Square Paths theorem for ∇ω(p_n) after circularly rearranging square paths and marking a return; the same methods give the t=1 Delta Square conjecture. In Section 7 the authors conjecture that ∇_*e_n is s⊗s-positive, checked by computer for n≤9, but s^{⊗k}-positivity fails in general for k>2 before specializing t=1; Theorem 5.8 restores q-positivity at t=1. They also conjecture m^{⊗k}-positivity of ∇_*^{k-1}e_n for general q,t and ask for a t-statistic on multi-labeled Dyck paths refining the t=1 area formula, pointing to Mellit and Carlsson-Mellit methods as a possible route.
why_relevant: Introduces a unified Macdonald-eigenoperator framework containing nabla, Delta, and Theta operators as specializations. Since superspace and bosonic-fermionic coinvariant formulas are expressed through these operators, this is useful for translating hook-coefficient identities into symmetric-function identities.
